coredata.dc:title: Lanthanide coordination polymers used for fluorescent ratiometric sensing of H2O2 and glucose
coredata.dc:creator: Zhu, Xinyue
Wu, Xinru
Liu, Jiaojiao
Zou, Lina
Ye, Baoxian
Li, Gaiping
coredata.dc:description: A dual-emission lanthanide coordination polymers (Ln CPs) were facilely prepared by self-assembly of guanine diphosphate (GDP) and 2-aminoterephthalic acid (ATA) with Tb3+, termed as Tb-ATA/GDP CPs. GDP played as the antenna ligand to sensitize the luminescence of Tb3+, and ATA served as a fluorescent auxiliary ligand for the ratiometric assay. The obtained Tb-ATA/GDP CPs exhibited both blue-emission of ATA at 430 nm and characteristic green-emission of Tb3+ under a single excitation wavelength. Upon addition of H2O2, the fluorescence of Tb3+ could be selectively quenched and that of ATA slightly enhanced. The fluorescence intensity ratio at 430 nm and 549 nm (I430/I549) increased linearly with increasing the concentration of H2O2 in the range of 0.005–20 μM and 120–500 μM, and the detection limit was 3.42 nM. Furthermore, by integrating the glucose oxidase (GOx) through the adaptive inclusion ability of Ln CPs, GOx@Tb-ATA/GDP CPs were facilely obtained. Based on their high affinity to oxidize glucose and the fluorescence response to the generated H2O2, a simple, highly selective and sensitive one-pot ratiometric glucose assay was developed. This work will facilitate the design and construct of multi-functional fluorescent probes based on the Ln CPs, and exploit their applications in bio-sensing and environmental analysis.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Ratiometric fluorescence sensor
Lanthanide coordination polymers
Single-wavelength excitation
Glucose
H2O2

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of Tb-ATA/GDP and GOx@Tb-ATA/GDP CPs
Design and characterization of Tb-ATA/GDP CPs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Fluorescence spectra of Tb-ATA/GMP, Tb-ATA/GDP, and Tb-ATA/GTP CPs.
Fluorescence spectra of ATA, Tb-GDP, and Tb-ATA/GDP CPs.
(A) SEM image of Tb-ATA/GDP CPs. (B) FT-IR spectra of GDP (a), Tb-ATA/GDP CPs (b), and ATA (c).
Fluorescence spectra of Tb-ATA/GDP CPs, Tb-ATA/GDP CPs + H2O2 (100 μM).

(A) Fluorescence emission spectra of Tb-ATA/GDP CPs under different excitation wavelengths. (B) Effect of pH on the fluorescence intensities of Tb-ATA/GDP CPs at 430 nm and 549 nm. (C) Fluorescence intensity ratios (I430/I549) of the sensing system versus reaction time.

(A) Fluorescence spectra of the Tb-ATA/GDP CPs in the presence of different concentrations of H2O2. (B) The linear relationship between the fluorescence intensity ratio (I430/I549) and the concentration of H2O2.

(A) Fluorescence spectra of the GOx@Tb-ATA/GDP CPs in the presence of different concentrations of glucose. (B) The linear relationship between the fluorescence intensity ratio (I430/I549) and the concentration of glucose.
The selectivity of the GOx@Tb-ATA/GDP CPs sensor toward glucose. The concentrations of Aga, Fru, Gal, and Suc are all 1 mM, and that of Mal, Man, and glucose are 100 μM.
Schematic description of the preparation of GOx@Tb-ATA/GDP CPs and detection of glucose.

head.ce:title: Lanthanide coordination polymers used for fluorescent ratiometric sensing of H2O2 and glucose

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry, Zhengzhou University, Zhengzhou 450001, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry, Zhengzhou University, Zhengzhou 450001, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Scheme 1. Schematic description of the preparation of GOx@Tb-ATA/GDP CPs and detection of glucose.

A dual-emission lanthanide coordination polymers (Ln CPs) were facilely prepared by self-assembly of guanine diphosphate (GDP) and 2-aminoterephthalic acid (ATA) with Tb3+, termed as Tb-ATA/GDP CPs. GDP played as the antenna ligand to sensitize the luminescence of Tb3+, and ATA served as a fluorescent auxiliary ligand for the ratiometric assay. The obtained Tb-ATA/GDP CPs exhibited both blue-emission of ATA at 430 nm and characteristic green-emission of Tb3+ under a single excitation wavelength. Upon addition of H2O2, the fluorescence of Tb3+ could be selectively quenched and that of ATA slightly enhanced. The fluorescence intensity ratio at 430 nm and 549 nm (I430/I549) increased linearly with increasing the concentration of H2O2 in the range of 0.005–20 μM and 120–500 μM, and the detection limit was 3.42 nM. Furthermore, by integrating the glucose oxidase (GOx) through the adaptive inclusion ability of Ln CPs, GOx@Tb-ATA/GDP CPs were facilely obtained. Based on their high affinity to oxidize glucose and the fluorescence response to the generated H2O2, a simple, highly selective and sensitive one-pot ratiometric glucose assay was developed. This work will facilitate the design and construct of multi-functional fluorescent probes based on the Ln CPs, and exploit their applications in bio-sensing and environmental analysis.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Lanthanide coordination polymers (Ln-CPs), highly sought-after hybrid materials, have been extensively applied in heterogeneous catalysis, biomedical imaging, and chemical sensing [1–3]. The infinite combination between various Ln metal ions (Ln3+) and bridge ligands endows the diversity of Ln-CPs in structure and chemical composition, while one of the most fascinating advantages of the Ln-CPs is their luminescent properties [4]. In the past few years, the design of functional Ln-CPs as luminescent probes for the sensing of biomolecules and ions had been widely reported [5–7]. Ln-CPs based fluorescence probes possess tremendous potential due to their unique optical properties, tunable structure, as well as the adaptive capability to encapsulate guest materials as a host platform [8–11]. It should be mentioned that, direct excitation of trivalent Ln3+ is always inefficient because of the forbidden electronic f-f transitions [12,13]. One general way to sensitize the luminescence of Ln3+ is to form the Ln CPs by selecting an appropriate organic ligand with a high molar absorptivity (known as “antenna ligand”), which can transfer the absorbed energy to the coordinated Ln3+ [14–17].

Up to now, various organic ligands have been explored for preparing Ln-CPs, such as phenanthridine, tetracycline, and 2,2′-dipyridyl (Bipy) [18–20]. Nevertheless, these organic ligands exhibited drawbacks including poor water-solubility, tedious fabrication, environmentally unfriendly, which limited the application of Ln-CPs for practical biosensing [21]. Since biomolecules are mostly water-soluble, and have multiple metal binding sites and self-assembly properties, the above problem can be solved by introducing biomolecules as ligands to replace organic ligands [22]. Some biomolecules like nucleotide molecules are commonly used as the alternative ligands to synthesize Ln-CPs [23,24]. Especially, guanine-containing nucleotide molecules are considered to be desired ligands to form Tb-based CPs, which is attributed to their effective sensitization to Tb3+ [25]. Regrettably, although several fluorescence sensors have been developed using guanine-containing nucleotide molecules as ligands, most of them are mainly based on single emission, which is vulnerable to external factors and particularly prone to resulting in low accuracy.

On the contrary, compared with the changes of single fluorescence intensity, the self-referencing ratiometric fluorescence strategy offers greatly improved accuracy and reliability of detection. It relies on the ratio of two fluorescence intensities as the output signal, and thus can largely eliminate the interference caused by the instrument and environment [26,27]. Moreover, the unique property makes them ready for practical applications. Although some luminescent materials, such as quantum dots and dyes, are easily encapsulated into Ln-CPs to construct ratiometric fluorescence probes, the leakage of guest materials may lead to instability of the probe [28,29]. Hence, an ideal dual-emission probe can be achieved by introducing a ligand of appropriate fluorescence and coordination properties to coordinate with Ln3+, and 2-aminoterephthalic acid (ATA) was proved to be a suitable candidate [30,31].

Enlightened by this, guanine diphosphate (GDP) and ATA were employed as two ligands to coordinate with Tb3+ through self-assembly to form CPs, termed as Tb-ATA/GDP CPs. GDP acted as the bridging ligand, which coordinated with central metal ion Tb3+ and further sensitized its luminescence through energy transfer. ATA played as the auxiliary ligand to provide the reference fluorescent signal for the ratio assay. Under the single wavelength irradiation, the Tb-ATA/GDP CPs displayed the characteristic emission of both ATA and Tb3+ with spectral distinguishable features. Interestingly, in the presence of H2O2, the fluorescence intensity of Tb3+ decreased significantly, while that of ATA increased gradually, enabling for ratiometric intensity-based H2O2 assay. Based on the fact that H2O2 is a substrate of glucose oxidation reaction catalyzed by glucose oxidase (GOx), we further encapsulated GOx into the Tb-ATA/GDP CPs via the adaptive capability, and developed a ratiometric fluorescent method for glucose detection, as shown in Scheme 1 .

Guanine monophosphate (GMP), guanine diphosphate (GDP), guanine triphosphate (GTP), glucose oxidase (GOx) and Bradford Protein Assay Kit were obtained from Sangon Biotech Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). N-2-hydroxyethylpiperazine-N’-ethanesulfonic acid (HEPES), sodium dodecyl sulfate (SDS), terbium nitrate pentahydrate (Tb(NO3)3·5H2O), glucose and glucose analogs were purchased from Aladin Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). 2-aminoterephthalic acid (ATA) was brought from Sigma-aldrich Co. Ltd. (America). Hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) was provided by Shuangshuang Chemicals Co., Ltd. (Yantai, China). All chemicals were of analytical grade and used as received without further purification. The ultrapure water was used throughout the study with an electrical resistance of 18.2 MΩ cm−1 (Thermo Fisher Scientific Co. Ltd., Shanghai, China).

The scanning electron microscopy (SEM) (Sigma 500, Gemini Japan) was used to characterize the morphologies. The fluorescence spectra were recorded on a 970CRT fluorescence spectrometer (Shanghai, China). The absorption spectra were measured at a Lambda 35 UV–vis spectrometer (Pgeneral Instrument, Beijing, China). HEPES buff solution (100 mM) was adjusted to different pH values through NaOH solution (1.0 M) with an FE28 digital pH meter (Mettler-Toledo, Shanghai, China).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of Tb-ATA/GDP and GOx@Tb-ATA/GDP CPs
ce:section.ce:para: All the CPs were synthesized through self-assembly process in HEPES buffer solution according to the previous reports [25]. Firstly, ATA aqueous solution (100 μL, 10 mM) and GDP aqueous solution (600 μL, 10 mM) were successively added into HEPES buffer solution (300 μL, 0.1 M, pH 7.4) under stirring. Secondly, 1 mL Tb(NO3)3 aqueous solution (10 mM) was added to the mixture and the resulting mixture was stirred for 3 h to form Tb-ATA/GDP CPs. The products were collected by centrifugation (10,000 rpm, 5 min) and washed three times with ultrapure water. Finally, the samples were re-suspended in 1 mL HEPES buffer solution (0.1 M, pH 7.4) and stored at 4 °C for further use.

The Tb-ATA/GMP and Tb-ATA/GTP CPs were prepared via the same procedure as described above by replacing GDP aqueous solution with the same concentration and volume of GMP and GTP aqueous solution respectively. The preparation of Tb/GXP CPs (X = M, D or T) was same as that of Tb-ATA/GXP CPs without adding ATA aqueous solution.

For synthesizing GOx@Tb-ATA/GDP CPs, GOX was encapsulated into Tb-ATA/GDP CPs during the self-assembly process. Similarly, 150 μL GOx (5 mg mL−1) was added in the HEPES buffer solution containing ATA and GDP. Other procedure was same as that of Tb-ATA/GTP CPs as depicted above.

The assay of H2O2 was described as follows: 10 μL of Tb-ATA/GDP ICPs stock solution was added into HEPES buffer (0.1 M, pH 7.4) and the resulting mixture was shaken well. Then, various concentrations of H2O2 aqueous solutions were added and the final volume of the reaction system was adjusted to 300 μL with HEPES buffer. After being allowed to react for 15 min in the dark at room temperature, the fluorescence spectra of the reaction solution were recorded accordingly upon 302 nm irradiation.

For the sensing of glucose, 10 μL of GOx@Tb-ATA/GDP CPs stock solution was added to the HEPES buffer (0.1 M, pH 7.4) containing glucose of different concentrations. The final volume of the reaction solution was kept to be 300 μL. After being mixed well and followed by reacting for 70 min at 37 °C, the fluorescence spectra of the reaction solution were determined at 302 nm excitation.

For the selectivity experiment, glucose was substituted with its analogs (including agarose (Aga), fructose (Fru), mannose (Man), maltose (Mal), galactose (Gal), and sucrose (Suc)), while the final concentrations of Man, Mal, and glucose are 100 μM, and that of other are 1 mM. The assay procedure was same to the above method for glucose.

Practicality was estimated with the real sample of human blood serum (HBS). The HBS sample was diluted with HEPES buffer (dilution 1:100) in advance. Various concentrations of glucose solution was added to make spiked HBS sample, which was then mixed with the GOx@Tb-ATA/GDP CPs suspension well. The subsequent procedure was as identical as depicted above.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Design and characterization of Tb-ATA/GDP CPs
ce:section.ce:para: Given the triplet-state energy of guanine matches the resonance energy levels of Tb3+, the efficient sensitization of guanine-containing nucleotide molecules to Tb3+ luminescence is demonstrated [32,33]. Besides, the luminescence intensity of Tb-CPs was also closely related to the number of phosphate groups, and the distance between the phosphate group and the luminescent center Tb3+. As reported, GDP and GTP revealed a stronger sensitized effect compared to GMP with monophosphate [34]. As a comparison, in this work, three guanine nucleotides (including GMP, GDP, and GTP) were selected to self-assemble with Tb3+ firstly. For the same concentration of Tb3+, the optimal concentration ratios of Tb3+ to GMP, GDP, and GTP were regulated to be 5:5, 5:3, and 5:2, respectively (Fig. S1). Among them, Tb-GDP CPs with the concentration ratio of 5:3 presented the strongest fluorescence, suggesting that GDP was the most efficient antenna ligand for sensitizing the luminescence of Tb3+ in these three kinds of CPs.

Subsequently, we introduced the second ligand ATA. Because of the rich coordination groups and inherent strong blue fluorescence, ATA was chosen as the auxiliary ligand to coordinate with Tb3+. As predicted, the luminescence intensity of the as-synthesized dual-ligand Tb3+-based CPs with different guanine nucleotides followed the order of GDP > GTP > GMP (Fig. 1 ). The weakest fluorescence of Tb3+ in Tb-ATA/GMP CPs was probably due to the poor rigid structure caused by the coordination of only one phosphate group with Tb3+ [35,36]. Of course, the coordination of three phosphate groups of GTP with Tb3+ increased the rigidity of Tb-GTP CPs, while the coordination of γ-phosphate groups led to a far distance between guanine and Tb3+ endowing the weaker fluorescence of Tb3+. Based on this, we chose the GDP molecule as the first ligand. To our knowledge, there are few reports on synthesis of Tb3+-based dual-ligand ratiometric fluorescent probes using GDP molecule as ligand, most of which were using GMP. As shown in Fig. 2 , ATA exhibited a fluorescence emission peak at 430 nm, which has a large spectral shift with the characteristic peaks of Tb-GDP CPs at 492 and 549 nm, allowing no spectral overlap of the two emitters. As expected, the as-formed Tb-ATA/GDP CPs displayed three well-separated emission peaks at 430, 492, and 549 nm (Fig. 2), which suggested that ATA and GDP successfully coordinated with Tb3+ to form a ratiometric fluorescent probe. Certainly, the Tb-ATA/GMP and Tb-ATA/GTP CPs exhibited similar luminous properties, but with a significantly weaker intensity.

The typical structure of obtained Tb-ATA/GDP CPs was recorded by SEM, which displayed adhered nanoparticles with an average diameter of 30 nm (Fig. 3 A). To further substantiate the coordination interaction, the FT-IR spectra of ATA, GDP and Tb-ATA/GDP CPs were recorded (Fig. 3B). For GDP, peaks at 1698, 1485, 1237, and 1111 cm−1 could be assigned to the P-OH stretching vibrations, the C8-N7 stretching vibration of guanine, the P-OH stretching vibrations and antisymmetric vibration, respectively. After the formation of Tb-ATA/GDP CPs, these corresponding peaks shifted slightly (1682, 1484, 1212, and 1104 cm−1) [37–39], which indicated that both the phosphate groups and nucleobase moieties of GDP chelated with Tb3+. As for ATA, there was a decrease of the N-H stretching vibrations of the amino group (3506 and 3393 cm−1), and a shift of C=O stretching vibrations of the carboxyl group (1690 cm−1) in the obtained Tb-ATA/GDP CPs, which indicated the successful coordination between ATA and Tb3+ [29,40]. These variations suggested both ATA and GDP chelated with Tb3+, and further confirmed the formation of Tb-ATA/GDP CPs.

To explore the response of the probe for H2O2, the fluorescence intensity change of Tb-ATA/GDP CPs after adding H2O2 was determined. As shown in Fig. 4 , upon addition of H2O2 to Tb-ATA/GDP CPs, the fluorescence of Tb3+ quenched drastically, while slight fluorescence recovery was observed for ATA. We also characterized the morphology of Tb-ATA/GDP CPs after reacting with H2O2, and no obvious change in morphology was found (Fig S2). H2O2, with both electrophilicity and nucleophilicity, might form weak hydrogen bonds and other forces with organic ligand, and then inhibit its energy transfer to Tb3+ [41]. The fluorescence changes of Tb-ATA/GDP CPs enabled the possibility for ratiometric assay for H2O2.

To achieve high-performance ratiometric fluorescence sensing of H2O2, some key experimental parameters, including the excitation wavelength, buffer pH value, and incubation time, were optimized. The fluorescence excitation profiles of ATA and Tb-GDP CPs were measured firstly for the determination of the excitation range of Tb-ATA/GDP CPs (Fig. S3). The excitation peaks at 267 nm and 345 nm were assigned to ATA, while Tb-GDP CPs showed a single excitation at 302 nm. As displayed in Fig. 5 A, the emission spectrum of Tb-ATA/GDP CPs was then recorded by exciting at wavelengths from 292 nm to 342 nm with 10 nm increment. With increasing wavelengths of irradiation, the fluorescence of ATA increased gradually, while the fluorescence of Tb3+ decreased when the excitation wavelength was longer than 302 nm. Upon excitation at 302 nm, Tb3+ exhibited the strongest emission, and ATA also emitted an acceptable fluorescence. Due to the detection of H2O2 being realized mainly by quenching the fluorescence of Tb3+, 302 nm was fixed as the optimal excitation wavelength.

Subsequently, the pH value of the HEPES buffer solution and the incubation time were investigated. Fig. 5B showed that the fluorescence intensity of Tb3+ at 549 nm increased with increasing pH and then dropped down when the pH exceeded 7.4, while the fluorescence intensity of ATA at 430 nm slightly increased in this pH range. Thus, for our ratiometric fluorescent probe, a HEPES buffer solution of pH 7.4 was the best choice. As for the incubation time of Tb-ATA/GDP CPs to H2O2 (Fig. 5C), the result showed that the fluorescence ratio I430/I549 increased significantly within the first 15 min, and then went slowly with time elapsing. Considering the instability of H2O2, we set the incubation time of the system at 15 min.

With optimized experimental parameters, we next investigated the detection performance of H2O2 based on the dual-emission probe Tb-ATA/GDP CPs. Fig. 6 A depicted the emission spectra of Tb-ATA/GDP CPs upon addition of varying concentrations of H2O2. With increasing the concentration of H2O2, the fluorescence intensity of ATA at 430 nm enhanced gradually, while that of Tb3+ at 492 nm and 549 nm decreased significantly. Based on the different fluorescence responses of ATA and Tb3+, the fluorescence intensity ratio at 430 nm and 549 nm (I430/I549) was taken as output signal for H2O2 assay. From Fig. 6B, it can be seen that an excellent linear relationship between the fluorescence intensity ratio (I430/I549) and H2O2 concentration in the range from 0.005–20 μM and 120–500 μM, respectively. The detection limit of 3.42 nM was calculated using 3σ (σ is the standard deviation of blank solution), which exhibited better performance than most optical methods reported previously for H2O2 assay (Table S1). Additionally, the ratiometric fluorescence probe of Tb-ATA/GDP CPs also possesses some nonnegligible advantages, including excellent luminescent property, simple preparation and easy operation.

Since glucose can be specifically oxidized by GOx to produce H2O2 in the presence of oxygen, we further developed a ratiometric method for glucose detection. The GOx@Tb-ATA/GDP CPs with catalytic and fluorescence properties were formed by encapsulating GOx in Tb-ATA/GDP CPs through the self-adapted assembly. As shown in Fig. S4A, the GOx@Tb-ATA/GDP CPs displayed a similar network morphology as that of Tb-ATA/GDP CPs, which indicated that the introduction of GOx did not have an obvious influence on the structure of its parent. This phenomenon was ascribed to the flexible rearrangement of Tb-ATA/GDP CPs during the self-assembly process [9].

The existence of GOx was validated by staining with Coomassie Brilliant Blue G-250. From Fig. S4B, upon the addition of the dye, GOx@Tb-ATA/GDP CPs showed a maximum absorption peak centered at 595 nm with a bright blue color. However, for Tb-ATA/GDP CPs, almost no absorption and color were observed. Apparently, this revealed the successful integration of GOx and CPs for consequently generating GOx@Tb-ATA/GDP CPs. Then, we further investigated the loading mode of GOx in Tb-ATA/GDP CPs by treating GOx@Tb-ATA/GDP CPs with excess sodium dodecyl sulfate (SDS). During this process, all of the surface-absorbed proteins will be removed, and which will affect the protein colorimetric assay. The results (Fig, S4C) showed that, after treating with SDS, the protein colorimetric assay of GOx@Tb-ATA/GDP CPs was not inhibited, while it can hardly be triggered by the mixture of GOx and Tb-ATA/GDP CPs. This indicated that GOx was loaded into Tb-ATA/GDP CPs rather than simply absorbed to the surface. As shown in Fig. S4D, the amounts of loaded GOx in Tb-ATA/GDP CPs were tested by using a Bradford Protein Assay Kit. Under the fitted calibration curve, the GOx loading efficacy was calculated to be 73.3%, which is evidently higher than those of the traditional hosts such as porous silica (∼15%) [42].

The effect of incubation time of glucose and GOx@Tb-ATA/GDP CPs was shown in Fig S5. The ratio I430/I549 increased with the incubation time and arrived at a steady value within 70 min. As expected, when the concentration of glucose increased, the fluorescence at 430 nm increased, accompanied by that at 492 nm and 549 nm decreased (Fig. 7 A). As depicted in Fig. 7B, an excellent linear relationship (R2 = 0.9948) between the fluorescence intensity ratio (I430/I549) and glucose concentration was obtained in the range of 0.01–150 μM with a detection limit of 8.69 nM (3σ, where σ is the standard deviation of blank solutions).

The selectivity of GOx@Tb-ATA/GDP CPs for glucose detection was assessed by observing the fluorescence responses of several glucose analogs such as Aga, Fru, Man, Mal, Gal, and Suc. The control experiment (Fig. 8 ) showed that the ratio I430/I549 hardly increased for these analogues, even when some control samples were used at concentrations as high as 1 mM, ten times that of glucose used herein. The results demonstrated the high specificity of the probe GOx@Tb-ATA/GDP CPs for sensing glucose, which may be owing to the high affinity of GOx to oxidize glucose.

To estimate the practical applicability of the proposed method, we further determined the glucose level in HBS samples. The recovery experiment was also performed through testing the glucose-spiked HBS samples. As shown in Table. S2, the recoveries for glucose in spiked HBS samples was from 97.26% to 100.08%, and the relative standard deviations (RSD) of all concentration levels were below 1.88%. Accordingly, the above results confirmed the reliability and practicality of this method for glucose assay in real samples.

In summary, we have constructed a dual-ligand coordinated fluorescent probe Tb-ATA/GDP CPs via a facile self-assembly, which could emit spectrally distinguishable emission bands of both ATA and Tb3+ under a single wavelength irradiation. Interestingly, H2O2 could lead to the inverse changes in luminescence intensity of the two emitters, and thus a ratio fluorescent sensor for H2O2 was developed. Furthermore, by integrating GOx through the adaptive inclusion property of CPs in the self-assembly process, a ratio fluorescent platform for simple, highly selective and sensitive one-pot ratiometric glucose assay was also developed. Theoretically, this universal design strategy should be able to expand to many other analysts, where H2O2 was a product of the biochemical reactions catalyzed by the proprietary enzymes. The flexible structures and components, as well as the facile synthesis and intrinsic properties of Ln-CPs, combing with the high affinity of the enzyme to corresponding substrate, may provide a new way for the design and development of ratio fluorescent sensor in bioassays.

Xinyue Zhu: Conceptualization, Methodology, Validation, Resources, Data curation, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. Xinru Wu: Formal analysis, Investigation. Jiaojiao Liu: Validation, Formal analysis, Methodology, Investigation. Lina Zou: Formal analysis, Methodology, Investigation. Baoxian Ye: Formal analysis, Methodology, Investigation, Project administration. Gaiping Li: Data curation, Writing – review & editing, Project administration.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 22176179).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.inoche.2022.109849.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

Y. Cui, Y. Yue, G. Qian, B. Chen, Luminescent Functional Metal–Organic Frameworks, Chemical Reviews, 112 (2012) 1126–1162.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A highly selective lanthanide-containing probe for ratiometric luminescence detection of an anthrax biomarker

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Lanthanide/nucleotide coordination polymers: an excellent host platform for encapsulating enzymes and fluorescent nanoparticles to enhance ratiometric sensing

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J. Gao, C. Wang, H. Tan, Lanthanide/nucleotide coordination polymers: an excellent host platform for encapsulating enzymes and fluorescent nanoparticles to enhance ratiometric sensing, Journal of Materials Chemistry B, 5 (2017) 7692–7700.
